tree.heading: A. Mortality

tree.content_preview: Complications may arise from all types of vascular access, though the nature, severity and frequency of complications varies between access types. The problems of catheter-related infection and venous

tree.content_preview: The concept of 'best access' informs standard clinician advice, but it is also an oversimplification which ignores knowledge uncertainties, patient variety and choice. This knowledge gap is increasing

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: Adequate time is required for the selection, formation, and maturation of dialysis access so that it is available when needed for dialysis initiation. In particular, when fistula access is planned, it

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: Long-term vascular access for haemodialysis can be provided by a venous catheter or AV access: creation of a fistula or placement of a graft. Ideally AV access should be easy to cannulate, minimally s

tree.heading: AV access location
tree.content_preview: The majority of fistulas are formed in one of three conventional locations, named according to the vessels from which they are formed: radio-cephalic (forearm), brachio-cephalic (upper arm), and brach

tree.heading: Grafts and thigh access
tree.content_preview: When vessels for conventional fistula formation have been utilised or are not suitable, graft insertion may be appropriate and should be considered. Since the graft itself is the conduit, no vein is r

tree.heading: Surgical and anaesthetic technique

tree.heading: Maturation
tree.content_preview: Once formed, regular assessment of AV access is important to detect complications, including dysfunction which may otherwise lead to access failure. Basic physical assessment using a 'look, feel and l

tree.heading: Cannulation
tree.content_preview: Cannulation should begin with an assessment of the access: a 'look, feel and listen' assessment is easy to complete prior to each cannulation to ascertain if the access is healthy or if there is cause

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: A proportion of fistulas and grafts develop dysfunction over time, which may manifest clinically as flow dysfunction, thrombosis, aneurysm, steal syndrome or a high flow state. The incidence of compli

tree.heading: Stenosis
tree.content_preview: Significant AV access circuit stenosis can manifest broadly as disorders of inflow (presenting with needling difficulty, inability to achieve flow or inadequate dialysis) or of outflow (presenting wit

tree.heading: Selection for angiography
tree.content_preview: Angiography is usually triggered when access stenosis is suspected due to clinical features (dialysis problems or examination findings) which may also be supported by haemodynamic monitoring (venous n

tree.heading: Angioplasty and outcome
tree.content_preview: There is no clear definition of the anatomic criteria for stenosis, but a clinically relevant stenosis reduces the luminal diameter by at least half, and usually much more than this, since lesser degr

tree.heading: The role of stents and drug-coated balloons
tree.content_preview: Though balloon dilation alone is usually successful, subsequent stent insertion is helpful in cases where rapid elastic recoil occurs, and stents may be better at preventing recurrent stenosis, at lea

tree.heading: Thrombosis
tree.content_preview: The most important consequence of AV access stenosis is thrombotic occlusion, and in most cases of access circuit occlusion there is a haemodynamically significant culprit stenosis. Fistula salvage th

tree.heading: Aneurysm
tree.content_preview: AV access aneurysms may occur adjacent to the arterial anastomosis, or more commonly along the cannulation segment, and may be focal (with one or two rounded expansions in an otherwise normal fistula)

tree.heading: Steal syndrome
tree.content_preview: 'Steal' is the clinical manifestation of distal ischaemia, developing as a consequence of the diversion of blood into the access, and therefore away from the hand and forearm, after access formation.

tree.heading: Catheter insertion
tree.content_preview: Tunnelled haemodialysis catheters are produced by multiple manufacturers and available in a variety of designs. Some are twin catheters, composed of two separate single lumen catheters which are inser

tree.heading: Catheter care
tree.content_preview: After insertion, catheter infection remains a constant risk, arising usually from contamination of the external or internal catheter surface, by organisms on the skin of patients or hands of staff. Nu

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: A proportion of catheters may develop complications over time, of which the most common are dysfunction (poor flow) and infection. As with AV access, much of the literature on catheter complications i

tree.heading: Catheter dysfunction
tree.content_preview: Tunnelled dialysis catheter dysfunction is a common problem, usually defined as inability of the catheter to deliver a blood flow of at least 300ml/min in adult patients. Dysfunction from the time of

tree.heading: Central vein thrombosis
tree.content_preview: External catheter-related thrombosis, occluding flow through the central veins, is a less common problem. This may present as face or arm swelling, but is often asymptomatic, found incidentally when i

tree.heading: Catheter-related infection
tree.content_preview: Tunnelled dialysis catheter infections are a significant cause of morbidity and mortality patients undergoing haemodialysis (14). Three clinical types of catheter infection are recognised: exit infect

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: Central venous stenosis (CVS) is defined as pathological narrowing or occlusion in one or more of the thoracic veins: subclavian, brachiocephalic (innominate) or superior vena cava (SVC), with simplif

tree.heading: 1. Mild CVS: non-symptomatic, with functional access
tree.content_preview: If CVS is clinically mild (no symptoms, with functional AV access) then no treatment is necessary, and may even be harmful. Most such CVS is not recognised and does not cause any detectable access dys

tree.heading: 2. Moderate CVS: symptoms or access dysfunction, but easy to open
tree.content_preview: Moderate CVS refers to cases with clinical features (symptoms or access dysfunction) but without difficulty opening the lesion, though the anatomy and nature of the underlying lesion varies widely bet

tree.heading: 3. Severe CVS: difficult to open, access usually dysfunctional
tree.content_preview: In severe CVS symptoms are very common in the presence of ipsilateral AV access, but variable with catheter access. These lesions are usually hard to open and most studies focus on the method used to

tree.heading: Opening the lesion
tree.content_preview: In most studies of this type of CVS, the focus is on successfully opening the lesion by first crossing it with a wire (so that the lesion can be dilated), often termed recanalisation, though some rese

tree.heading: Subsequent access
tree.content_preview: A common access after treatment of severe CVS is a catheter through the lesion. Subsequent symptoms are uncommon and the catheter facilitates opening the lesion in the event of a requirement for retre

tree.heading: 4. Occlusive CVS: non-SVC access required
tree.content_preview: This section deals with thoracic CVS which is bilaterally occlusive, so that non-thoracic access is therefore required. Occlusive is a variably used term, since clearly some CVS is described as occlus

tree.heading: Unconventional and experimental options
tree.content_preview: A number of less common approaches have also been described with reasonable outcome, though one should remember that small studies of uncommon treatments are particularly prone to publication bias. Th

tree.heading: Terms which quantify the effects of disease and treatments
tree.content_preview: Significance . How sure we can be that this effect is 'real' and not just a chance observation. This depends mostly on the size (number of participants) of the study. For example, if you toss a coin 5

tree.heading: Other statistical concepts
tree.content_preview: Bias . Any way in which a study is not 'fair'. This is not the same as 'noise' (unknowns which make the true effect hard to see) because bias refers to things which tend to push the effect artificiall

tree.heading: Audit measure 8: A yearly survey of cannulation practice and miscannulation.
tree.content_preview:
Data should be collected for one dialysis session for a random sample of patients, including:

Needling Technique (as per BRS VA/VASBI Definitions)

Number of patients using each needling technique fo

tree.heading: Missed Cannulation
tree.content_preview:
Number of patients for that haemodialysis session that experienced more than one attempt to insert a needle at one needling site.

More than one attempt is defined as either:
Complete removal and the

tree.heading: Aud it measure 9: A yearly survey of patients' experience of access.
tree.content_preview: Questionnaires should be collected from a random sample of patients, for example using the VASQoL questionnaire, currently being updated by the authors.
